metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the parent-wins conflict on `criteria_routing.json`: the parent (6982) states 'R2/R3/R4 each touch `criteria_routing.json`' but R2's plan explicitly excludes any `criteria_routing.json` change — update the parent first to correct R2's stated file-impact boundary (removing R2 from the criteria_routing.json serialization group), then re-review R2 against the corrected parent, rather than silently diverging the leaf.
evidence: Parent 6982 Context: 'R2/R3/R4 each touch criteria_routing.json and land serially in any order (file-impact keeps them out of one batch)'
R2 Out-of-scope: 'Any criteria_routing.json change (refuted by the code, see above)'
R2 Design decisions: 'It EXTENDS p6_ac_quality; it does NOT add a P10 and does NOT touch criteria_routing.json'
R2's file_impact list (from the ticket) does NOT include criteria_routing.json

The parent's serialization constraint for R2 rests on an assumption (criteria_routing.json overlap) that R2 refutes — the constraint may still apply for R3/R4, but the parent's stated reason for including R2 in it is incorrect
findings.finding: The parent's ordering section characterizes R2 as one of the stories that touches `criteria_routing.json` ('R2/R3/R4 each touch criteria_routing.json and land serially in any order'), but R2's plan explicitly and repeatedly says it does NOT touch `criteria_routing.json` at all — this is a direct scope-boundary contradiction where the leaf diverges from the parent's description of what R2 does.
findings.location: Parent (6982-2e75-f8fa-43b9) Context section: 'R2/R3/R4 each touch criteria_routing.json and land serially in any order (file-impact keeps them out of one batch)'; R2 leaf Out-of-scope section: 'Any criteria_routing.json change (refuted by the code, see above)'; R2 leaf Design decisions: 'It EXTENDS p6_ac_quality; it does NOT add a P10 and does NOT touch criteria_routing.json'
scenarios: An implementer reading the parent to understand ordering constraints would conclude R2 must land serially with R3/R4 because of criteria_routing.json conflicts — but R2 has no such conflict, potentially causing unnecessary sequencing delays
A reviewer auditing the parent's file-impact model would find a stated R2 scope (criteria_routing.json) that the leaf plan contradicts — creating ambiguity about which description is authoritative
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent 6982's Context/ordering section to separate R2 from the criteria_routing.json serialization constraint (e.g. 'R3/R4 each touch criteria_routing.json and land serially; R2 has no file-overlap with R3/R4 and can land in parallel'), which stales the parent's plan-review attestation and forces its re-review per the G7 protocol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `passes.py:769` citation in the 'Single-source the tag matcher' paragraph — `enrich_operator_attested` is called at `workflow_ops.py:434`, not `passes.py:769`; verify the import-cycle argument holds for the actual call-site.
evidence: Plan text: 'consumed by the completion-verifier enrichment path (workflow_ops.py:80 enrich_operator_attested, passes.py:769)'
Search result: enrich_operator_attested only appears in workflow_ops.py at lines 80 and 434; passes.py search returns no matches
passes.py:769 contains coaching-move '9' template text ('plan the verification'), not an enrichment call
findings.finding: The plan asserts `enrich_operator_attested` is consumed at `passes.py:769`, but a search of passes.py finds no call to `enrich_operator_attested` there — it is called at `workflow_ops.py:434`. The Design Decisions section states the 'completion-verifier enrichment path (`workflow_ops.py:80 enrich_operator_attested`, `passes.py:769`)' but passes.py:769 contains coaching-move template text, not an enrichment call.
findings.location: Design decisions — 'Single-source the tag matcher' paragraph; claim 'passes.py:769'
scenarios: A developer following the plan to trace the import chain starts at passes.py:769, finds unrelated coaching-move code, and mis-models the dependency graph for the single-source refactor
If the plan's rationale for why det_floor must own the regex (to avoid an import cycle through passes.py) rests on a wrong call-site, the import-cycle argument may be mis-specified
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'passes.py:769' with 'workflow_ops.py:434' in the Design Decisions paragraph. The import-cycle rationale is unaffected since workflow_ops already lazy-imports det_floor at :135, confirming the ownership direction is correct regardless of line number.

evidence: verify command `grep -qi "operator-attested" docs/plan-review-gate.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Self-gate section that `operator_attested_ac_corpus.jsonl` is produced by extracting AC text from the tickets in `outcome_corpus.jsonl`, and document the extraction methodology (how AC items are fetched from the store) so the '3091 AC items / 31 tagged' statistics are independently reproducible.
evidence: outcome_corpus.jsonl lines sampled: each row is {ticket_id, ticket_type, post_claim_edit_class, reopen_count, …} — no AC item text
Search for 'operator_attested_ac_corpus' and 'operator_attested_eval' in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/ returns no matches — files do not yet exist
Plan claims: 'outcome_corpus.jsonl → 3091 AC items, 31 already tagged [operator-attested]' but this derivation requires a separate AC-extraction step not recorded in the existing file
findings.finding: The plan asserts the frozen corpus is `outcome_corpus.jsonl` ('556 E1 outcome-corpus tickets that resolve in the store … 3091 AC items, 31 already tagged [operator-attested]'), but `outcome_corpus.jsonl` contains per-ticket metadata rows only (no AC item text), making the '3091 AC items' and '31 already tagged' counts unverifiable from the stated file. The eval harness and corpus JSONL named in Scope (`operator_attested_ac_corpus.jsonl`) do not yet exist, so the plan's self-gate numbers rest on an unverifiable statistical claim about a file whose structure does not hold AC text.
scenarios: A reviewer or CI gate tries to reproduce the '31 tagged' recall denominator and cannot locate the source data, undermining trust in the self-gate numbers

If the actual AC-extraction step over outcome_corpus tickets yields a different count of tagged items, the precision/recall figures in the committed eval.json would be wrong and the gate threshold claim unsupported

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (and `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` if the file isn't already listed there) to the Scope section, noting that the `det_floor.py` ceiling must be bumped to accommodate the new regex, lexicon, and helper (~30–60 additional lines).
evidence: .github/module-size-ceilings.txt line 41: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_floor.py 975`
det_floor.py currently ends at line 975 (det_coverage function)
Plan adds _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE regex, an operational-evidence lexicon (marker families + suppression + negation patterns), and the _operator_evidence_ac_gaps() helper to det_floor.py
tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py enforces the ceiling via SET EQUALITY against the ceilings file
Scope section lists only: det_floor.py, workflow_ops.py, harness/corpus/eval files, the new unit test, docs/plan-review-gate.md
findings.finding: det_floor.py is at its pinned module-size ceiling of 975 lines (.github/module-size-ceilings.txt). Adding _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE, the operational-evidence lexicon, and _operator_evidence_ac_gaps() will push it past 975, breaking test_module_size_contract.py and the CI shell gate. Neither .github/module-size-ceilings.txt nor .github/module-size-allowlist.txt is listed in the plan's Scope section.
scenarios: make test fails on test_module_size_contract.py because det_floor.py exceeds its 975-line ceiling
The CI Verified vote fails on the module-size shell gate in gerrit-verify.yaml
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` — bump the `det_floor.py` line ceiling from 975 to the landed size (estimated 1010–1040 after adding the regex + lexicon + helper).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Design decisions / Scope / AC#4 whether `workflow_ops` must re-export `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` under the OLD alias `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` (for backward compat and to satisfy the verify command), or update the verify command and all callers to use the new name.
evidence: Design: 'det_floor will OWN the regex as `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` and `workflow_ops` will import it'
AC#4 verify: 'from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b'
Design does NOT state that the old `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` attribute name is preserved in workflow_ops as an alias
findings.finding: The Design decisions section says `workflow_ops` will 'import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from `det_floor`' (drop the local duplicate), but AC#4's verify command imports it from `workflow_ops` under the OLD name `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` (`from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b`). An implementer who simply does `from det_floor import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` in `workflow_ops` (as the design literally says) without also preserving/aliasing the old name would break the AC#4 verify command at runtime.
findings.location: Design decisions section ('Single-source the tag matcher') vs AC#4 verify command
scenarios: Implementer imports new name only into workflow_ops; AC#4 verify command raises AttributeError on `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE`
Downstream callers of `workflow_ops._OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` (e.g. passes.py:769 path) break silently if the old name is dropped
findings.suggested_fix: In the `workflow_ops.py` scope bullet, explicitly state: 'replace the local definition with `from .det_floor import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` so the public name is preserved and the AC#4 identity assertion holds.' Alternatively, update AC#4's verify command to import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from both modules under the same name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope bullet for `workflow_ops.py` that `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` is **re-exported as an alias** (`_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE = _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE`) rather than deleted, and add an acknowledgement that any direct importers of the old name are either updated or covered by the alias.
evidence: "import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from det_floor (drop the local duplicate)" (Scope)
"from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b" (AC item 4 proof) — requires the old name to remain importable from workflow_ops
Absence of explicit statement that `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` is re-exported as an alias rather than deleted
findings.finding: The Scope section says workflow_ops should 'import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from det_floor (drop the local duplicate)', but AC item 4's proof command requires `from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b` to succeed — implying the old name must survive as a re-export alias. If 'drop the local duplicate' is implemented as a straight deletion (no re-export), any existing consumer of `workflow_ops._OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` breaks with no migration path or acknowledgement.
findings.location: Scope section — `workflow_ops.py` bullet; AC item 4 proof command
scenarios: A module outside det_floor/workflow_ops that does `from workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` (e.g. passes.py:769 or test code) gets an ImportError after the change lands.
The AC item 4 proof itself fails if the name is truly dropped rather than re-aliased.
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow_ops.py scope bullet, replace 'drop the local duplicate' with: 'replace the local definition with `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE = _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` (alias preserved for existing importers; the regex object is shared, so `a is b` holds for AC item 4)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope bullet for `workflow_ops.py` that `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` is **kept as a re-export alias** (`_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE = _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE`) rather than deleted, so existing importers (test files, AC#4 proof command) remain intact.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from det_floor (drop the local duplicate)'
workflow_ops.py:61 defines `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE = re.compile(...)` — the existing name

tests/unit/test_plan_review.py:338: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import operator_attested_ac_texts` and line 356 import `enrich_operator_attested` — both delegate to the same module-level regex
tests/unit/test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py:8-9 imports both `enrich_operator_attested` and `operator_attested_ac_texts` from `workflow_ops`
AC#4 proof: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b` — requires the OLD name to remain importable at workflow_ops
Plan Design decisions section: 'workflow_ops will import it — no import cycle, one source of truth' — does not address aliasing the old name
findings.finding: The Scope bullet for `workflow_ops.py` says 'drop the local duplicate' but does not specify whether the existing `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` name is deleted or kept as a re-export alias. At least two test files (`tests/unit/test_plan_review.py:338`, `tests/unit/test_a8e5_operator_attested_edge.py:8-9`) import `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` by that name from `workflow_ops`, and AC#4's own proof command requires `from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b` to succeed. A straight deletion (no alias) would silently break all existing importers of the old name — the plan neither acknowledges these consumers nor states that a re-export alias will be left in place.
findings.location: Scope section — `workflow_ops.py` bullet; AC item 4 proof command
scenarios: Developer implements 'drop the local duplicate' as a straight `del` / removal; existing test imports of `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` from `workflow_ops` raise `ImportError` at collection time.
The AC#4 proof command (`… import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b …`) itself fails, making the acceptance criterion self-defeating unless an alias is explicitly added.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'drop the local duplicate' in the workflow_ops.py Scope bullet with: 'replace the local `re.compile(…)` definition with a re-export alias `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE = _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` (preserving the existing public name for current consumers while making det_floor the single source of truth)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the 'Single-source the tag matcher' design decision that `workflow_ops` must bind `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` as a **module-level** name (e.g. `from .det_floor import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` at the top of the file) so the `assert a is b` proof command in AC #4 can access it as a module attribute.
evidence: Plan: 'det_floor will OWN the regex as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE and workflow_ops will import it'
AC #4 proof: 'from rebar.llm.plan_review.workflow_ops import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE as b; assert a is b'
workflow_ops.py:135: 'from . import det_floor, orchestrator' — existing lazy/local import convention in this module
ABSENCE: the plan never explicitly states the import in workflow_ops must be at module level (top of file), not inside a function body
findings.finding: The plan says `workflow_ops` imports `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` from `det_floor` but does not specify that this import must be at MODULE LEVEL (not inside a function body). The AC proof `assert a is b` requires `workflow_ops._OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` to be a module-level name bound to exactly the same compiled object — achievable only via a top-level `from .det_floor import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE`. An implementer who follows `workflow_ops`'s existing convention of lazy intra-function imports (as seen at line 135) would place the import inside `operator_attested_ac_texts()`, making `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` absent as a module attribute and breaking the proof command with an `ImportError`.
findings.location: Design decisions / 'Single-source the tag matcher' section; AC #4 proof command
scenarios: Implementer follows the existing workflow_ops convention of lazy imports, places 'from .det_floor import _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE' inside operator_attested_ac_texts(); the module-level name _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE does not exist; the AC proof command fails with AttributeError/ImportError.

Implementer adds a top-level import (correct), but the change from lazy to eager now makes det_floor a static import-time dependency of workflow_ops — a behavioral change the plan does not acknowledge, leaving reviewers uncertain whether this is intentional.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the design decision: 'This is a top-level (module-level) import in workflow_ops, replacing the current module-level `re.compile(…)` definition — not a lazy local import — so that `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` remains accessible as a module attribute (required by the `assert a is b` proof).'
